data.title: Par Pacific Holdings: When It's Actually Not Too Good To Be True
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Par Pacific Holdings (PARR) has risen over 113% at the time of writing this. Most might close the tab as soon as they see this, but I’m here to suggest there is still more long-term value as an owner. The investment thesis I have is simple. PARR benefits from Canadian oil falling as it widens the crack spread and means more earnings are generated. However, even if the gap closes the core segments are still sufficient to support continued aggressive buybacks. On top of this, the company is already trading at a cheap valuation. I’m starting a position here as I also do my first coverage on the stock.

Parr Pacific - Company Overview

PARR is divided into three very distinct operating segments. Perhaps the most well-known being the Refining segment, responsible for the absolute majority of all of the revenues along with the operating income. The past quarter, Refining produced 95% of all the operation income, which is why it will of course be the main topic of today's article.

But besides this segment, PARR also has Logistics and Retail. The first of these consists of a large set of various services, running from crude storage, transportation, marine terminals to single-point mooring. It's a very diverse segment, but one that has been on a strong growth trajectory. Back in 2019 it reported $42.35 million in operating income during the first 9 months, a figure that has now grown to $75.81 million instead. Much of this growth came in 2023 when the company acquired assets from ExxonMobil (XOM). These assets consisted of both refineries (the Billings) and also some logistics assets that got included in this segment of PARR.

Lastly the retail segment covers the more wholesale side - think gas stations, convenience stores, for example. Despite the volatility that often comes with crude oil and integrated downstream companies, this segment in particular has performed very well over the years, growing the operating income from $36.5 million to $55.8 million between 2019 and 2025 (measured in the first 9 months). But more important, the operating margins have also grown from 10.6% in 2019 to now 12.8% instead.

To conclude, PARR runs on a business model that consists of acquiring and developing various downstream assets along with running a vast network of logistics in the energy business and a “frontend” wholesale segment as well. As opposed to other energy companies that might provide investor value through large dividends or distributions, PARR has instead over the past years started buying back vast amounts of shares instead, something that has helped the stock price surge over the past 12 months. Just the past 9 months it spent $97.2 million on buybacks, equivalent to 5.1% of the company's current market cap.

Refining - The Cash Cow Of PARR

So for the first segment we have of course the refining operations. These are covered in four different states: Hawaii, Washington, Wyoming and Montana. Hawaii is the largest and the only major refinery on the island. It operates similarly to the others, and many of the refineries share some commonalities in the end products they make. What sets the Hawaii refinery apart is in particular the sourcing of the feedstock (crude oil). Because of the location it actually receives most of it on a global basis, so to speak. The others have all access to large US oilfields like Bakken or Powder River Basin, and if not, they purchase from Canada.

I've compiled some of the most important measuring points for these refineries, and we can see that it has overall seen a decent throughput growth YoY, or at the very least stable. Yield is slightly down, but this is explained by the short downtime at the Wyoming refinery.

Our Wyoming refinery experienced an operational incident on the evening of February 12, 2025, and remained safely idled during repair and recovery work through late April 2025, when the refinery returned to full crude operations.

If fully operational, the likelihood is that PARR would have reported rising yields YoY. What I also want to point out is that the Washington refinery is currently the only major asphalt producer in the Pacific Northwest. The price of asphalt has been trending down over the past few months and forecasts suggest this trend will continue further. In terms of product yield, asphalt makes up about 18.6% of the production in Washington, flat YoY.

It is quite impressive just how fast PARR has been able to pay back the cost of acquiring the Billings refinery in Montana. The cost for the refinery and the other assets, including the logistics earlier mentioned was $310 million. Since the operations should have normalized right now after being under PARR for a little over 2 years, I think we can look at the 2025 numbers to estimate the return that the company actually got here. With a throughput of about 56 - 58,000 bpd x 365 gets us about 20.8 million barrels per year. During the first nine months the gross margin per bbl was $18.5. This suggests $384 million in gross contributions from the Montana refinery alone. PARR basically paid below what the annual contributions were for the location. I want to make it clear that this is before other expense lines like depreciation, corporate G&A or interest expenses.

Now why could that be possible? I mean it’s great for PARR, and its investors will have cheered on as the stock price is up about 66% since the announcement on June 1, 2023 ($22.34 was the share price then).

The press release from XOM included the following in context to the asset sale:

ExxonMobil is focused on investing in facilities where we can manufacture higher-value products such as lubricants and chemicals

To me it looks like PARR got the better of the deal here, and it’s been reflected in how the stock price has moved since.

I couldn't write this article without covering one of the main reasons it’s even able to drive these high earnings: SRE or “Small Refinery Exemption” For those not aware, the Renewable Fuel Standards or RFS requires that refiners like PARR blend renewable fuels into gasoline or buy compliance credits instead which are called RINs. This is actually an entire industry in itself. I covered Clean Energy Fuels (CLNE) not that long ago, and their entire business model relies on selling these credits.

But smaller refiners, often under 75,000 bpd, can petition to be exempt from this requirement. The three mainland refineries it has of course all qualify for this, which is why it has started being reported on the income statements now.

In August 2025, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (“EPA”) granted our mainland refineries a combination of full (100%) and partial (50%) small refinery exemptions (“SREs”) from the RFS program for the 2019 through 2024 compliance years.

When the EPA granted PARR a complete or near exemption, it opened up a lot of earnings growth because of the 6-year backlog it had. The EPA basically gave the RINs back and PARR could then turn around and sell these at the current market price. The $203 windfall was received because of this, measured as the difference between the previous purchase and the current value, is of course not recurring. Instead the SREs will be granted annually and are done case by case. This is why measuring the performance of PARR without accounting the SRE is the best approach.

The key takeaway here from the table is that yes the SRE has had a serious impact on the companies earnings and will continue to provide. But there are some strong points here as well, like the Washington operations for instance. It has core margins of $5.3 when not accounting for the SRE benefits. This helps it contribute with $76 million in annualized net income.

Hawaii on the other hand is the more reliable cash cow for PARR where there of course are no SRE impacts but it’s still pumping out $122 million in net income over the past 9 months for the company. The Wyoming figures are distorted by the one-time incident at the refinery in early 2025. The Montana refinery is still under optimization and I think will be a major winner in the long term. Excluding the SRE benefits it still has an annual run rate of $18.745 million. Going back to the price tag for the Billing acquisitions, this implies a payback period of around 4.1 years. That is very fast, and we are more than halfway there already.

Cash Flow Breakdown - Can PARR Keep Buying Back Shares

For calculating the net income I’ve applied a 24% tax rate for both segments. PARR doesn't break this out in its filings, but for the sake of this I think it's a fair number to use. We can see that the net income has risen sharply over the past 6 years. This comes in part because of the Billet acquisition again. Trying to not sound repetitive, but this was a major deal for all segments.

The question I have here is how well can PARR sustain the current buybacks. Based on these two segments alone, very well, it seems. Combined with the earlier shown figures for the refineries, it seems clear the PARR can continue to aggressively buy back shares. Since the introduction of this, the share count has declined by 15%, or 5% per year.

The current valuation of PARR does suggest a pretty steep discount given the incredibly resilient earning profile I’ve presented so far. Even if the more volatile operations of Refineries turn negative or crack spreads tighten more, then other two segments can still provide a lot of value. They contribute about $133 million in net income, which puts PARR at a relatively cheap FWD P/E of 14x.

Accounting for the two strongest refineries, Hawaii and Washington, we are looking at another $239 million to the mix. Since these are “extraordinary” times and the likelihood is high that spreads would come down, I’ll apply a 25% discount to this (refinery net income). This still gets me $312 million in net income. This implies that PARR trades at 5.99x FWD earnings. For a company focusing on buying back large amounts of shares and a strong core foundation of assets, I think this is an absolute steal to be buying at, even after the run it has had the past year.

Risks

The main risks with any downstream company like PARR, and I’ll refer to it like this since the majority of the operations are refinery-based, means that it’s bound by the crack spread as for how much earnings it can derive in a certain period.

Because so much of the feedstock is coming from Canada, PARR experienced a much wider crack spread last quarter and for the better part of 2025. This is largely due to the discount that Canadian oil has been trading at compared to other benchmarks. This doesn't necessarily impact the end price that PARR charges for the various products it makes. But what can change and most likely will change is that the spread could tighten and that would immediately impact the margins of PARR. This seemed to be the case until recent events in Venezuela. The buzz was that the US was going to open up the Venezuelan oil market to the world, sending the price of Canadian heavy oil even lower - the lowest in 18 months in fact.

This news suggests that PARR could continue to have a less expensive feedstock using Canadian oil and that the discount will continue. But, and I want to stress this point, the news is still developing, and none of the major oil companies have said a figure, and some haven’t even commented on whether they’ll invest or not. I think the market got ahead of itself, as it often does, and we’ll see an uptick in the Canadian oil price again. The effects here are that the price of PARR could fall drastically depending on the recovery.

The Full Picture

Investing in PARR should come with the knowledge that there will likely be a lot of volatility because it’s just the nature with most downstream companies. However, PARR has the advantage of operating other assets too in logistics and retail, which covers the base when the crack spread for the references are smaller. It’s the complete package, and that's why I’m also rating it a strong buy now.

data.title: Palantir: Simplifying The Investment Decision
data.description:
Much of the narrative for Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR) revolves around the verbal bull-bear tug-of-war that pits some very compelling performance against an equally compelling counterbalance of challenging valuations.

We have all seen the best of bull arguments here on Seeking Alpha - the proprietary tech angle, the strength of its commercial segment growth, its linchpin role in enhancing government agencies' capabilities, the stickiness of these government contracts, the agentic nature of its software layer, the almost-incredible margins it reports quarter after quarter... one quickly gets the idea.

On the other side of the debate are equally well-worn but equally valid objections such as untenable valuation levels, a high level of insider selling (particularly in 2025), analysts predicting growth rate slowdowns in 2026, and the 'glorified consultancy' nature of the business that makes it different from a conventional SaaS company, and what have you.

All said and done, one's investment decision will typically be based on which side does a better job of convincing one. The problem is, each side only offers the overtly obvious facts, which do nothing to convince the other side of their validity. You may have noticed, but most bullish or bearish articles focus excessively on the point being made and less on telling the other side of the story - the "what if I am wrong?" part.

To avoid falling into that trap, I prefer to look below the surface, underneath the headline figures. What this often reveals is the true balance of a business's strengths against its weaknesses.

Rather than inform me that the business is growing and margins are expanding or that valuations are extreme, and the downside risk is expanding, it reveals to me some far more valuable insights, such as the quality of that growth, the sustainability of those margins, the forward growth visibility, etc.; on the other side, the context of the valuation metric being used, the inability of traditional valuation methodologies to keep up with emergent technologies, and such.

Palantir Through The Lenses Of Risk And Reward

My approach to analysis is always from a risk perspective, followed by the quality and sustainability of growth. To me, the question "how much and what type of risk is involved?" comes before "can this give me returns on a long-term basis?" For Palantir, I shall modify those to the following:

  1. What level of risk do current PLTR valuations pose to a potential gain on one's investment?
  2. Do the catalysts and drivers of the eventual reward exhibit signs of longevity?

Our next objective is to expand on the two queries posed above and see how that applies to an investment in PLTR.

Risk Assessment For PLTR's Valuation

We shall begin with PLTR's challenging valuation because this is ultimately where the risk arises. First, a look at Seeking Alpha's data on PLTR valuation metrics.

Well, no wonder the bears bring this up as their biggest supporting character in their narratives! On the surface, these valuations are ridiculous. Who would pay 100 times forward sales for any stock?

The reality, however, is that someone did pay for the stock to go from 30 times forward sales to 60 times to 90 times and well over 100 times before strong revenue estimates for FY 2026 finally brought them down to the current level of under 70 times forward sales. One should note that Seeking Alpha's P/S (forward) figure of 95 uses the FY 2025 revenue estimate of $4.41 billion in the denominator, but the YCharts data below uses the FY 2026 consensus estimate of $6.26 billion, which puts it at 67 times those revenues.

What I am trying to convey here is this: what seems like sheer bullish buying force pushing valuations up to that 100+ level is actually being supported by forward revenue estimates. Many investors would happily pay 67 times next year's earnings; even better, one could buy PLTR now for 48 times FY 2027 earnings. The farther out you go, the cheaper it gets, and this is just from growth estimates in the 20% to 50% range.

Naturally, one would have to consider the very limited number of analysts that are projecting beyond FY 2027, so we must seek further validation.

There is some merit to these estimates based on historical revenue growth. The above annual revenue increase table shows a strong upward cadence over the past three fiscal years. An even clearer picture emerges in the quarterly view.

The revenue increases certainly seem well-sustained since the lows of FY 2023, and are now firmly entrenched, lending credibility to those forward estimates from Street analysts.

To sum up this revenue risk study, I would posit that although there is no denying the audacity of the market in pushing up sales multiples to this degree, there is sufficient evidence to suggest that forward estimates are, in fact, supported by actual current performance.

If you recall, our first line of inquiry was, "What level of risk do current PLTR valuations pose to a potential gain on one's investment?"

We have now answered that question. We know that the stock may have outrun the underlying business performance, but that realistic forward estimates mitigate that risk to a high degree.

And, in case one were wondering about the equally ridiculous P/E (forward, adjusted) ratio that is nearly 10 times that of the sector median, or around 245 times adjusted earnings, a similar pattern emerges there - annual and quarterly margin growth rates look highly sustainable and in line with forward estimates.

Of course, this precludes any short-term investment strategies, so one must necessarily stay invested for the long run in order to overcome the valuation risk that PLTR currently faces.

On to our second question: "Do the catalysts and drivers of the eventual reward exhibit signs of longevity."

Reward Assessment For PLTR's Upside Potential

This is where we look at revenue sources, their longevity, and their potential for profit margin expansion. We have already seen the strength of revenue growth and of margin expansion, but we do need to see clear potential upsides in their operational efficiencies.

As we run up to Q4 2025 results early next month, let us look at any demonstrable trends over the past two reported quarters.

In Q2 2025, we saw U.S. Commercial revenue growth touch 93 percent, with U.S. Government revenue growth at a strong 53 percent, both year-on-year. It was also the quarter in which Palantir crossed $1 billion in quarterly revenues. Q2 also saw over $900 million in new deals, and guidance was raised to $4.15 for FY 2025.

Step forward one quarter to Q3 2025, and U.S. Commercial revenue growth stood at 121 percent, with U.S. Government revenues holding steady at 52 percent, both year-on-year. New deals were at $1.2 billion, and guidance was raised again, this time to $4.4 billion, which is what the Street has agreed on ahead of Q4 earnings.

This is exactly the kind of quality growth an investor wants to see in their investment because it shows that demand is strong and on the rise, revenue visibility is excellent (hence the parity between Street estimates and management guidance).

In terms of segment performance, Commercial seems on fire, and for good reason. Several reasons, actually.

The most important of these is the go-to-market nature of its AIP Bootcamp. This crucial customer touchpoint is absent in most SaaS offerings. By deeply integrating the relationship between the company, the product, and the customer from day one, and allowing clients to see the power of AI in action in as little as five days is nothing short of revolutionary in the world of enterprise software deployment.

Is it a coincidence that the launch of the AIP in 2023 coincides with the trough in Palantir's revenue growth profile? I think not. Palantir may not be an innovator in the field of AI, but it was certainly a fast-mover when LLMs made their public debut in late 2022 with ChatGPT. Palantir not only had the foresight to develop a product associated with strong commercial demand, but also the aggressiveness to lock in their relationships with new clients with in-person interaction. The fact that it allows a client to go from zero AIP knowledge to building a real-world solution in what Palantir claims is "a matter of hours."

...where customers are hands-on-keyboard and achieving outcomes with AI in a matter of hours — the key differentiator is a software architecture which revolves around the Palantir Ontology.

This Ontology is the most crucial component, in my opinion because it creates a digital representation or "twin" that acts as a language or a translation tool that users can intuitively learn and use to build use cases. The Ontology allows the user to visualize and make sense of otherwise meaningless data, but it goes far beyond that, and lets them make decisions on the fly without having to deal with the data itself.

This is AI at its most commercially viable, in my opinion. I see these as practical solutions that almost guarantee long-term recurring income, and this is precisely what is responsible for the 121 percent U.S. Commercial revenue growth in Q3 2025, and it is what will drive this segment's and overall revenues over the next decade.

In many ways, U.S. Commercial represents the growth facet of Palantir, so where do U.S. Government revenues fit in? Naturally, the value facet - the anchor for its "ridiculous" valuation. The important takeaway from Q3 is the government's commitment to gaining an AI edge over other nations, and this is something the CTO Shyam Sankar highlighted on the conference call:

The U.S. Army issued an official public memo directing all army organizations to consolidate and centralize on Vantage, the army data platform built on foundry and AIP. The army views this, not merely as a technical decision, but a cultural decision, enabling the data-driven decision-making that continues to make our army the most lethal in the world. This directive will enable the army to rapidly sunset legacy systems and enable more investment in the army's future force concept and systems.

The importance of this cannot be overstated because it almost guarantees long-term entrenchment for Palantir.

Coming to upside potential, there is unfortunately no benchmark comparison one can make here to value this type of business model, the rapid adoption of the software platforms it offers, and the deep penetration into both the commercial and government sectors.

So, how do we gauge upside potential? A DCF cannot help here because growth rates at the top and at the unlevered free cash flow levels are continually increasing. We have already seen that comparative valuations make PLTR stock look extremely bloated. What is the solution, then, if there is no way to arrive at a fair price?

What I Would Suggest

My recommendation here is to carefully weigh the risk against the reward, and go with the side that seems more compelling to you. Once again, we arrive back at the same destination point - which side convinces you more? That is the side you should be on because investing is more about conviction than anything else. You can conduct the most thorough due diligence and still get it wrong, which is why I think the importance of conviction is highly understated.

To me, the unknown upside potential is far more compelling than the risks and the long duration for this investment to properly play out. I do not see these strong growth rates subsiding over the next five to ten years, at the very least. Palantir's suite of AI products is at the leading edge of enterprise AI adoption, and even without support from government contracts, this would be a compelling investment. With them, the opportunity is undeniable.

Palantir's current and estimated growth virtually represent, in a very muted manner, how quickly AI adoption is happening in the enterprise world, and the depth of each relationship that Palantir builds with its clientele is attested to by how fast their ACV metric is moving up for some of its larger clients. The deeper it goes, the longer the relationship because Palantir's solutions are rapidly becoming an integral part of these systems, replacing legacy business intelligence with a new paradigm based on AI. That is as strong a long-term lock-in as one is likely to get with what is essentially a SaaS product suite.

To end with, I would say that PLTR is a Strong Buy regardless of how it is currently valued because we cannot know the upside potential for a business that offers a revolutionary turkey solution to C-suite decision-making, AND is additionally entrenched with defense systems to give it unparalleled stability. What one gets with PLTR is long-term growth and value packaged in an expensive-looking bundle. One should not be fooled by the packaging; instead, rip open the valuation and look closely at what underpins it all, as we have done in this analysis. One side certainly looks more convincing than the other, does it not?

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Paramount Skydance Takes Fight To Warner's Board To Block Netflix Deal
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Paramount Skydance nominating its own Warner directors. (0:15) Ex-Fed chiefs defend Jerome Powell as DOJ subpoenas roil independence. (1:12) Credit card issuers slide after Trump touts 10% rate cap. (2:13)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, Paramount is making its move on Warner’s board.

Paramount Skydance (PSKY) says it plans to nominate directors to Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) and has filed suit in Delaware seeking basic information. It says Warner shareholders need to make “an informed decision” between its bid and the current deal with Netflix (NFLX).

In a letter to the Warner board, Paramount said it will also propose a bylaw amendment requiring WBD shareholder approval for any separation of Global Networks.

“We do not undertake any of these actions lightly,” the letter said. “Make no mistake, our goal remains to have constructive discussions with WBD's board to reach an agreement that is in the best interests of WBD shareholders.”

Adding political pressure, President Donald Trump signaled his disapproval of Netflix gaining control, reposting an opinion piece titled “Stop the Netflix Cultural Takeover.”

Even so, odds of Netflix acquiring WBD are holding steady on prediction markets. On Kalshi, the chances stand at 54%, while Polymarket puts the odds at 53%.

Back to the battle in the nation’s capital, several former economic officials, including Alan Greenspan, Ben Bernanke, Hank Paulson, and Janet Yellen, issued a joint statement calling the investigation into Jerome Powell and the Fed “an unprecedented attempt to use prosecutorial attacks to undermine” central bank independence.

“This is how monetary policy is made in emerging markets with weak institutions, with highly negative consequences for inflation and the functioning of their economies more broadly,” they said. "It has no place in the United States, whose greatest strength is the rule of law, which is at the foundation of our economic success."

And in the world of unintended consequences, the odds of Powell leaving the central bank after his chairmanship ends have tumbled.

Powell’s term as Fed chair ends in May, but he’ll still have two years left as a governor. He hasn’t said whether he’ll stay or go, but speculation is that after the subpoenas, he could remain to fight for Fed independence.

On Kalshi, the proposition “Jerome Powell out as Fed Governor before August 2026?” has dropped to below 50%, from 85% on Saturday.

Among active stocks, echoing a story from Wall Street Brunch, credit card and issuer stocks are slumping after President Trump called for a one-year cap on card rates at 10%. Among the biggest decliners are Capital One (COF), Synchrony Financial (SYF), Bread Financial (BFH) and American Express (AXP).

Abercrombie & Fitch (ANF) is plunging after full-year sales guidance of 6% growth looked a bit light for a stock that’s rallied sharply since the end of November.

And UnitedHealth (UNH) is down after The Wall Street Journal reported that a Senate committee found it used “aggressive tactics” to collect payment-boosting diagnoses for its Medicare Advantage members. Shares are off their lows, though, after the company affirmed its outlook.

In other news of note, Eli Lilly (LLY) will partner with Nvidia (NVDA) on an AI co-innovation lab in the San Francisco Bay Area aimed at using AI to accelerate drug discovery.

Lilly scientists will work alongside Nvidia AI model builders and engineers “to generate large-scale data and build powerful AI models that can accelerate medicine development,” with Nvidia’s BioNeMo platform central to the effort.

The two companies plan to invest up to $1B in talent and infrastructure over five years.

data.title: NCR Voyix: Wait For Solid Results
data.description:
I have seen many NCR Voyix (VYX) types in my life. Every one of them had the potential to cost me. There is always a turnaround coming. Or maybe the next change will do the job. There was always something coming that would make the future better than the past. I honestly hope that is the case now. But given the company record over the last few decades, I would wait for a track record of results that one would expect from a software focused company before I invest.

This common stock price history is not one that would lend itself to a buy and hold strategy unless you wanted to lose money over the long term. There have been times when the stock definitely went up. But the stock never went up long enough to sustain a higher value. As the above chart shows, the stock always gave back gains and now is hitting lower lows.

Finances

Software stocks are supposed to grow in the double-digit range and print money in the process.

But this company has a fair amount of debt. Yet the stock price history demonstrates that the debt is not an advantage. That would very much imply some cost control or even quality control issues that are causing some margin issues to get to insufficient profitability and cash flow. There is no reason for a software focused company to have that debt ratio. In fact, there is no reason for a software company to have debt period.

Guidance

Furthermore, the guidance for the fiscal year is not all that encouraging.

Note that the key software and services revenue is adjusted essentially towards the lower end of guidance (and potentially then some). The last article noted that this is where the company is focusing the business. One would have expected some progress since the last article. In the last article, I didn't like the lack of progress back then. I still don't think that the progress supposedly made is anything close enough to adequate for an investment consideration.

The hardware business is effectively shipped out of the corporation with the company remaining (effectively) a dealer of the hardware.

Then there is that free cash flow. That probably needs to be at least doubled the level it is at.

Given what I am seeing, there is absolutely no reason to commit money to this idea at the current time. Even the earnings per share, while that is improving on the latest guidance, it is likely to hardly start a sustained stock price rally.

Income

One of the issues that I have with the income statement is a lack of income.

There is an old saying on Wall Street: "never buy a stock that is losing money because it might lose more money". There is a reason for that stock price action shown above and it appears to be a lack of profitability that one might have expected for a company of this type.

History

I have followed this company a very long time. NCR stood for (originally) National Cash Register. Now obviously those days are gone. But there was the transition to contesting market leader Diebold (DBD) for what was then the brand-new ATM market.

We are long past that as well. But now comes the news that the hardware will be made by someone else with the company focusing on software. But the profitability shown above as adjusted is something less than breathtaking.

For as long as I can remember, there has always been a reason, for the performance of this stock. It was always going to get better and stay better.

Given the decades I have followed this stock and the slick presentations I have dealt with, I would probably get out the popcorn and soda and watch this until there is a track record that entices me to invest.

Management

For me, good management outperforms when its least expected and it always performs well when it is expected to perform well. This management would appear to have a history that is the polar opposite.

Overall Results

As a result, there really is no evidence of growth as shown below in the year-to-date figures.

Things like EBITDA and of course earnings can only improve so much if revenue is not growing. As shown above, clearly revenue is not really growing. Software focused companies are generally expected to grow revenue in the double-digit range. There is nothing close to that shown above.

Therefore, I would wait for a solid revenue growth track record before I would consider an investment. Software is generally a fast-moving industry that requires a fair amount of investment to keep competitive. Therefore, wide margins and decent cash flow are a must as part of that "keeping competitive". This situation, on the other hand, appears to be a fairly tight situation.

Summary

NCR Voyix remains a strong sell idea. There may be a turnaround here as some recent press releases would at least suggest. But there has been nothing in the past history to indicate that a turnaround, if it even exists, would be sustained.

Therefore, I will get out the popcorn and soda and watch this from the sidelines because it is way easier on my portfolio than having an investment position.

I definitely do not like the debt shown above for a software focused company no matter the reason for it. I also do not like the ratio of gross debt to free cash flow.

The lack of revenue growth is also an area of concern as well as the recent losses.

I have honestly followed this too long for me to go with any possible investment thesis for this idea. This management (actually various managements) has tried various things through the years without real results.

There was a time when this company had a decent track record. I would again wait for that track record to appear before I invested any money at all.

Risks

There is always the possibility that this time, the company will sustain a turnaround, and the stock price becomes that homerun idea that so many have wished for over the decades I have followed this stock. To all those people I would say good luck with that idea. I honestly hope it happens.

But the key for me is that good management is generally investable at any time. It is rarely too late to get on board with good management. Here, this stock would benefit traders with discipline that can walk away with a profit. But that makes this stock unsuitable for the average buy and hold investor.

There is also the possibility that management can increase free cash flow; then the debt ratio comes down and that debt gets paid. But again, I would wait for that to happen. This company has had more chances to do well than I can remember over the five decades I have followed it.

data.title: Progressive: A Long-Term Compounder Built On Underwriting Discipline
data.description:
Progressive (PGR) is one of those insurance carriers you love to have in your portfolio. Richly valued? Yes (but - spoiler alert - not currently). Overlooked? Probably not. But underappreciated? In 2025, with no doubt.

In my latest company's review, I pointed out several company strengths:

  • A steady and resilient underwriting margin performance, with a 10-year combined ratio of 92.2%.
  • This steady underwriting performance was coupled with a double-digit premium growth. Progressive's gross written premiums increased from $19.6 billion in 2014 to $65.5 billion in 2024.
  • This same underwriting performance was mostly fueled by strict cost monitoring, resulting in an expense ratio below 20%, like GEICO, one of Berkshire Hathaway's (BRK.A)(BRK.B) insurance subsidiaries.
  • A home insurance business, which was ramping up after several years of losses. This presence in the homeowners' insurance market was an additional asset for Progressive to increase the policyholders' retention.

Yet despite all that, the stock performance was negative in 2025.

Blame the rotation, blame rate cuts, blame anything - but the fundamentals haven't changed. November data showed continued strengths despite investors' lack of attention since the second part of the year.

11M2025 Results: Steady Results, Strong Signals

Some investors might say PGR could do more, deliver better, aim higher. Maybe. But in 2024, as noted by A.M. Best, Progressive was already the king of the motor insurance business:

Progressive Insurance Group ascended to the top of the U.S. total automobile insurance ranking with a 16.4% market share in 2024, just edging out former No. 1 State Farm Group's 16.2% market share, according to a new Best's Ranking.

In 2024, no one could match Progressive’s consistency, pricing accuracy, and scale. Not even the usual heavyweights like Chubb (CB), Allstate (ALL), or GEICO. And for 2025, the same results can be expected.

For the first 11 months of 2025, Progressive reported:

  • $74.5 billion in net earned premiums, up 16% year-over-year

  • 12% growth in net written premiums, led by motor insurance

  • Continued margin discipline, with underwriting income up significantly across all segments. The year-to-date combined ratio was 87.5%, vs. 89.3%, or a 1.8-point improvement.

Lob-wise, the property lines have shown the largest performance improvement.

LoB Nov 2024 Nov 2025
Property Lines 103.7% 87.4%
Commercial Lines 89.9% 88.0%
Personal Lines (excl. Property) 88.3% 87.9%

Still, the impact on underwriting income was immaterial, since property lines only made up $2.8 billion of the total $74.5 billion in earned premiums.

Thanks to the underwriting margin improvement, the premium growth, and the higher investment income ($3.3 billion vs. $2.6 billion in 11M2024), the post-tax income grew from $7.5 billion to $10.2 billion.

Mr. Market may have overlooked these results, expecting even more from Progressive. To me, the 11M2025 results were steady. Plus, the company has declared, as every year, a special dividend. For FY2025, the special dividend is $13.5 per share. Based on the current stock price, it represents a 5.9% yield, in addition to the current tiny regular dividend (a dime per share and per quarter - it will not pay for your coffee).

What Can You Expect for December?

I do expect December results to be higher than last year in the same period, thanks to higher investment income, fueled by the higher asset base and a steady combined ratio.

In December 2024, the company reported monthly income of $942 million, with a combined ratio of 84.1% and a net earned premium level of $6.7 billion. Assuming 10–15% growth, December 2025 premiums should reach between $7.4 and $7.7 billion.

With a combined ratio between 85% and 95% (let’s be conservative), pre-tax underwriting income could range from $370 million to $1.15 billion. Add $260–280 million in investment income, and you get to a post-tax income range of $500 million to $1.1 billion, assuming a 21% tax rate.

Not bad for a month, especially when the market didn’t seem to care.

And for FY2026?

Let's be transparent, I don't have a crystal ball. I can't foresee the future. Even though Nostradamus and I share a birthplace (France - and no, I don’t wear a beret or eat frog legs, but my English accent is terrible), I can’t foresee the future.

Fortunately, many financial service firms are happy to make predictions for us. Well-known names used to publish reports and forecasts, such as:

  • Fitch, regularly publishing its insurance outlook - including insights for 2026.
  • Deloitte, which released its perspective on the U.S. and global insurance markets for the coming years.
  • Swiss Re (SSREF)(SSREY), through its Sigma reports, shares long-term views on global insurance demand, pricing cycles, and capital trends. The 2026 outlook report also includes additional economic metrics, like GDP forecasts.

Rather than reinvent the wheel, I’ll rely on their work to provide my own 2026 forecast for Progressive. For 2026, Deloitte’s latest outlook expects the following:

The property and casualty (P&C) insurance segment seems to be moving beyond a prolonged hard cycle into a period of margin pressure and slower premium growth

In plain English (and for those who are not insurance fetishists like me): Competition is rising, pricing momentum is fading, and cost pressures are building; Deloitte expects deterioration in the loss ratio, impacting the non-life insurance companies’ profitability. For the North American region, Deloitte only forecasts a low-single-digit premium growth.

The chart captures the key point well: Real premium growth is expected to slow across most regions into 2026, with advanced markets suffering from lower premium growth.

That doesn’t mean Progressive can’t execute - it just means the backdrop may shift from “easy mode” (strong rate tailwinds) to a more normal ("challenging" “challenging,” to be precise, the most pessimistic forecasters) environment where underwriting discipline and cost control matter even more.

In 2026, the social inflation will still be there; the severity trend will certainly increase. Consequently, the combined ratio might rise, resulting in reduced underwriting income.

Over the last three years, Progressive's combined ratio improved significantly, dropping in 2024 below 90%.

2024 was an outlier year - in a good way. 2025 tends to be as good as 2024. The question for investors is what “normal” looks like from here.

If competition intensifies and social inflation remains sticky, margins could (and surely will) compress. The most impacted underwriting metric would be the loss ratio (incurred claims divided by earned premiums) as Progressive tends to be a company that impressively controls its internal costs, with an over-the-cycle expense ratio ranging around 20%.

In that setup, with a loss ratio jumping to the 70-75% area, it would result in a normalized combined ratio oscillating between 90 and 95%.

A 5% to 10% underwriting margin would still be very respectable - especially for an auto-heavy book - but it would be a clear step down from 2024 and 2025’s peak execution.

Based on an FY2026 premium volume of circa $89 billion, an underwriting margin ranging from 5% to 10%, the FY2026 pre-tax underwriting income might land between $4.4 and $8.9 billion. Adding $3.9 billion of investment income, the post-tax income might range from $6.6 to $10.1 billion.

An Acquisition Potential?

Progressive’s most recent acquisition was Protective in 2021 (for roughly $340 million). Historically, the company has avoided large deals and has relied mostly on organic growth.

With a current market share of more than 15% in motor insurance (16.7% in personal insurance market and 14.99% in commercial insurance market in 2024), M&A opportunities appear to be limited, so any external growth angle would more likely come from Property/Homeowners. That said, property insurance business is a tougher arena: Catastrophe exposure is structural, competition is intense, and margins can swing fast. The motor insurance business is a challenging market, property insurance segment is challenging too; for both segments, the key to success is the scaling effect.

The U.S. homeowners market is large (about $162 billion in 2024), but the competition is very real. Progressive remains a small player for this market, which is dominated by mutuals and larger listed companies, like Allstate, Travelers (TRV) or Chubb. The first six largest players held more than 50% of the market. The top 10 own more than 60% of the total market, and Progressive is only the 12th largest U.S. property insurer.

With a relatively small market share, it took Progressive years to get its small property insurance division to something close to breakeven.

2025 would certainly be another profitable year, even though the underwriting margin of its property business would be lower than its motor insurance segment. While I would like to see Progressive push further into homeowners, I don’t think M&A is the obvious path.

Mercury General (MCY) is a name that occasionally comes up, but Progressive acquiring MCY is likely unlikely for some reasons:

  1. California market concentration: Mercury is overwhelmingly exposed to California - a difficult market where regulation, pricing pressure, and wildfire-driven volatility have pushed many carriers to shrink their footprint over the prior years. That dynamic intensified in 2025 after the January wildfires.
  2. Control structure: Mercury’s shareholder base is effectively locked: The Joseph family controls roughly 51% of the equity, which makes any friendly transaction structurally harder.
  3. Valuation timing: The stock has rerated sharply in 2025 (ca. +44%), meaning any deal would likely require a premium on top of an already-recovered valuation. If Progressive had wanted to buy MCY, the window arguably looked more attractive one or two years ago, when profitability was under pressure, and the price reflected it.

Progressive could also acquire non-listed competitors, but investors shouldn’t count on M&A to fuel Progressive’s growth. If the company keeps growing at a double-digit pace, it will mostly come from rate actions and policy growth through its distribution networks - not from acquisitions. This management decision is certainly healthier, as Progressive will decide on the underwriting and pricing processes, resulting in better underwriting performance.

A Few Words To Conclude

Even if the market didn’t reward Progressive in 2025, the fundamentals remained intact. PGR is still one of the best-in-class U.S. P&C insurers, with a dominant position in the motor insurance segment, a disciplined underwriting process, and a track record of profitable growth that very few peers can match.

At roughly 11.6x forward earnings, Progressive doesn’t look richly priced for that level of quality - a double digit RoE, a 15.5% debt-to-capital ratio, and double-digit premium growth with a steady position in the motor insurance market. A couple of years ago, investors were willing to pay 18–20x for the “growth + consistency” combo. Today’s multiple reflects a more cautious backdrop, not a weaker company. Investors prefer focusing their efforts on fancier names, AI-related companies, and tech-oriented firms. Insurance is boring, and Progressive, despite all its strengths, is penalized by the sector in which it evolves.

Over the long run, PGR remains a solid core holding for investors who want exposure to the U.S. P&C insurance market without taking unnecessary execution risk. Some investors may prefer “disruptive” stories such as Lemonade (LMND), but that comes with a very different risk profile: Growth is not the same thing as profitability, and underwriting discipline still matters.

Progressive may not be exciting - but in insurance, boring is often exactly what you want.

data.title: Dole: A Deeply Undervalued Way To Invest In Real Food
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Dole plc (DOLE) offers excellent exposure to real food, still trading at a deep discount to their fair value even if we account for a higher margin of safety, standing to benefit from long-term trends and even policies that are part of Make America Healthy Again, among others.

The company relies mostly on their bananas and pineapples categories, which represent ~45% of their sales, while also having exposure to several other fruits and vegetables, including melons, citrus, berries, etc. Similar to my analysis of Fresh Del Monte Produce (FDP) - which I also rated a Strong Buy recently - the investment idea behind this type of company is very simple and straightforward.

In an environment that keeps being pushed more and more against ultraprocessed foods, they offer a solid and vertically-integrated way to gain exposure to real food, with farms, packhouses, land and even ships that back these operations, becoming a more and more important strategic asset that might become attractive for the market in the future.

The company still holds the #1 position in bananas across the US and Europe and #2 in pineapples, being among the largest in its industry, which is an advantage that can offer several opportunities, as I’ll mention in the Risks & Opportunities section below.

Dole’s free cash flow was negative in the first 9 months of 2025, reporting -$66.16 million compared to $49.46 million in 9M’24, although we should note that Q3 came with $66.5 million in free cash flow, while most of the change in cash provided by operating activities comes from working capital changes and the capital expenditures were well above the level from 9M’24.

If we look at their 2024 annual report, however, we can see how more than half of the net cash provided by operations came in Q4, signaling their significant seasonality, with the company’s management even noting their “typical seasonal working capital trend” during the Q3 Earnings Call.

As we can see in the table above, Dole still managed to make ~$180.29 million in FCF in 2024, $220.56 million in 2023 and $238.05 million in 2022, with significant divestitures in 2024 that continued into 2025.

Similar to FDP, Dole has been going through several divestitures over the past couple of years, including selling its 65% equity stake in Progressive Produce in early 2024, their fresh vegetables division in mid-2025, and even their Port in Guayaquil, Ecuador in December 2025 for $75 million in net proceeds (not included in the reports yet), which is part of Dole's plan to divest lower-margin/non-core infrastructure assets while focusing on its core strengths and higher-margin opportunities.

As for the guidance, Dole is targeting reaching the upper end of their $380 million to $390 million Adjusted EBITDA range (in line with 2024's $392.2 million and 2023's $385.1 million), planning to allocate capital to high-return development projects but reducing their FY25 CAPEX guidance for continuing operations to $85 million compared to $100 million before.

Financially, based on Dole’s latest report, we see an overall good position, with the current assets covering their current liabilities, with an overall manageable amount of long-term debt and a solid amount of cash and cash equivalents available.

Given the $1.38 billion equity attributable to the shareholders, we’re currently at a PB of around 1, which is not bad, but we have to balance the fact that they include significant goodwill and even their DOLE brand (at acquisition value).

Dividends & Buybacks

Dole pays a ~2.4% dividend yield that costs about $32.4 million per year, which is far from an issue given the free cash flow, for a ~17.97% payout ratio based on 2024’s free cash flow, which is far from an issue.

Back in November 2025, Dole’ Board authorized a $100 million buyback program with no set expiration date, which they plan to use from time to time at their discretion, for a total yield of ~7.4%.

Realistically, the company can spend even more than this per year and still have money left for investing and dividends, but the timing/volume of these buybacks remains up to the management.

Risks & Opportunities

I’ll note again the relatively weak consumer environment that can be bad especially for the higher-end/premium or niche produce that Dole sells, as consumers are forced to tighten the belt and potentially focus only on the pure essentials.

As mentioned before, several recent studies highlight the significant pressure that’s still felt by the US consumer due to factors including job insecurity, lower income and rising costs, with uncertainty around inflation still looming.

Meanwhile, we also have a significant industry-wide issue caused by the Tropical Race 4 (TR4) in Ecuador (confirmed recently; the country prepared for its inevitable arrival for years) and Black Sigatoka in regions like Costa Rica are causing significant volume losses, and even though Dole didn't offer that much info about it, FDP has been very vocal in saying that the industry is "losing the battle" against TR4, forcing them into an expensive containment mode that's affecting volumes.

Combined with heavy rains, Costa Rica’s banana exports dropped by ~20.7% in the first half of 2025, for example, which only adds to the severity of the situation.

Meanwhile, TR4 is a really big problem, as once it contaminates a farm, the soil is contaminated for decades, basically having the potential to take entire farms out of the game overnight.

While the market is focusing on the booms in copper, gold or silver, we might soon face a global banana crisis if these aren’t contained, with the big producers like Dole even working on TR4-resistant variants for years. FDP’s CEO even gave really worrying signals during their Q3 Earnings Release:

I mean, the disease has -- no matter what you do, it's like a losing battle against that disease. I mean you can replant and then 3 years later, 4 years later, you lose 3 again. So this is really -- people don't understand and realize how serious this issue is. And this is going to happen, be it tomorrow or after a year or 2 or 3 is going to happen. It's going to come. And I can assure you that, that disease does not stop spreads. It's just a matter of time. (...) And I think for bananas, in particular, there will come a time that people realize that there is not enough bananas in the market and the prices will shoot up in a way that will be a shock to the market.

Meanwhile, tariffs can put further pressure on the major producers, as even though bananas are currently exempt from tariffs (they were initially, but these were rolled back), we have to remember that Dole is more than just bananas, being exposed to issues like the tariffs on Mexico, as the company has various production facilities across the Americas, South Africa and Europe.

An opportunity that Dole also highlighted comes from their size, which opens the potential for them to buy peers in an effort to consolidate that can be sustained by the banana trouble, while being able to get better deals and deliver solid accretion thanks to their industry-leading brand.

Especially thanks to their recent asset sales, the company still makes significant amounts of money that can be used to direct cash acquisitions (debt is also available), while having the advantage of being a public company that opens the potential for stock-based acquisitions, which is an advantage over private peers.

Meanwhile, the relatively “tiny” market cap of ~$1.35 billion also opens the possibility for them to be the buying target for a food giant. Especially with all the Make America Healthy policies that can hurt ADM (ADM) or Bunge (BG) quite a bit, these companies may be forced to look into “real” food as a way to hedge their processing businesses, while also having the funds, logistics and size to dominate the industry.

Again, especially given the MAHA policies plus potential for other changes, this type of company can stand to benefit from significant attention from the market and even larger players from the broader food industry (or private equity), with MAHA policies potentially leading to a boost in demand for the company’s produce.

Meanwhile, the recent San Francisco lawsuit against 10 of the world’s largest food and drink producers can further support it, potentially “forcing” money to migrate into other food plays in an effort to at least diversify this defensive exposure and hedge the political risk.

Risks Reiterated

Although the company is in a good position and offers very solid exposure to its industry, they are still depending on macro factors, and the warnings given by FDP’s CEO can cause very significant volatility to their key segment.

On top of the contamination risk that can lead to volatility that can cause an unpredictable reaction from the market, there’s also the risk stemming from tariffs, which add yet another layer of unpredictability to the mix.

Valuation

Assuming that working capital normalizes in Q4 and CAPEX reaches their target, let's use $150 million in free cash flow in 2025 as a base for a better margin of safety (~20% below 2024’s level to account for macro pressure and CAPEX). I'll assume a 4% CAGR over the first 5 years thanks to their higher-margin investments and inflation, followed by a 3% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a conservative 2.25% terminal growth rate, as well as a 10% discount rate to take into account the higher risks due to TR4 and 95.98 million shares outstanding as of their Q3 report.

Even this gets us to an equity value of $2.18 billion and an intrinsic value of $22.70 per share, which is well above the current $14.34, while the company also stands to benefit from potential volatility.

Conclusions

Dole is a Strong Buy, backed by strong financials and a very attractive valuation, trading at a deep discount while offering exposure to a vital industry. There are risks and volatility should be likely in case TR4 advances and causes disruptions in the supply chain, but the current level still offers a very good entry level for me, and I placed a buy order shortly before writing the analysis.

data.title: Franklin Municipal Ladder 1-7 Year SMA Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: In the third quarter of 2025 (Q3), new issuance of municipal (muni) bonds exceeded the average seen in the past few years, driven by a 34% year-over-year surge in July issuance. By quarter end, the yield on the benchmark 10- year US Treasury (UST) note fell by eight basis points (bps) to 4.15%, while the 30-year UST yield decreased by four bps to 4.73%. US markets rose on clearer tariff policies, economic resilience, and a more dovish Fed, which cut rates by 25 bps to 4.00%-4.25% in September.
  • Contributors: Overweight overall duration.
  • Detractors: Overweight general obligation (GO) bonds. Overweight higher-quality muni bonds.
  • Outlook: Municipal bond valuations continue to appear attractive, particularly on a tax-adjusted basis. In a more challenging environment, strong bottom-up research and disciplined security selection will be essential to identifying relative value and preserving portfolio quality.

Performance Review

  • An overweight to GO bonds curbed returns for the period as these bonds performed worse than revenue-related issues during the period.
  • The strategy’s tilt toward higher-rated securities hindered results as they tended to have lower returns than lower-quality muni bonds.
  • The strategy’s overweight to duration contributed to relative performance, led by its overweight allocation to bonds with five years to maturity.

Outlook

  • Muni bond issuance exceeded historical averages in Q3 as issuers, who had been reluctant to issue new bonds under the looming threat to their tax-exempt status, came to market. Positive fund flows into the asset class were not able to keep up with the supply. The muni bond yield curve continues to steepen, with longer-maturity bond valuations cheapening somewhat.
  • Fundamentals remain broadly stable, however, as the economy slows and inflationary pressures ease, tax revenue growth at the state and local level is expected to moderate, placing greater importance on credit selectivity going forward.
  • Municipal bond valuations continue to appear attractive, particularly on a tax-adjusted basis. Yields are near multi-year highs, allowing investors to lock in compelling income. In a more challenging environment, strong bottom-up research and disciplined security selection will be essential to identifying relative value and preserving portfolio quality.

data.title: eBay: Improving And Fully Re-Rated
data.description:
By year-end 2023, I concluded that cheap was not enough in the case of eBay Inc. (EBAY). Amidst stagnant revenues and earnings, and some key engagement metrics on the decline, a cheap valuation and plan to simply buy back stock felt not enough to get involved.

Cheap valuations in the end provided a foundation for a meaningful outperformance, although some heavy lifting was done by the business as well, which has seen accelerating growth. Subsequently, shares have more than doubled from levels in the low forties to the $90 mark here, as eBay has delivered and convinced investors along the road.

This means that shares have risen for the right reasons, but the valuation multiple has re-rated substantially, which makes me much more reserved now, as further triggers here require more and continued growth for longer.

eBay: The Marketplace

eBay is known as a marketplace for consumers and businesses (both acting as buyers and sellers) to make a wide variety of transactions between consumers, with businesses, or between businesses. The platform remains dominant for peer-to-peer and SMB trade, including 134 million active buyers, about $80 billion in gross merchandise volume, including truly global operations.

This is easily seen if we look at the financial numbers over the past decade, as revenues have risen by just about two billion to just over $10 billion, with this growth not keeping up with inflation. Meanwhile, the company has seen quite stable operating margins in the mid-twenties, as the real achievement has been unheard-of buybacks, with the share count down over 60% over the past decade.

The lack of operating performance invited Elliott Management to get involved in 2019, pushing eBay to sell some business assets along the way, as that has been part of the reason for the shortfall in sales. This included a more than $9 billion sale of eBay's Classified business, after which eBay sold its stake in the Korean business in another $3 billion deal as well. The proceeds came on top of net cash balances held by the business already.

2023 Perspective

With reported results held back by the divestment of the abovementioned assets, the company saw some stagnation. In November 2023, the company reported a 2% increase in third quarter GMV to $18 billion, with quarterly revenues up 5% to $2.50 billion and earnings just topping the $1 per share mark.

Factoring in the fair value of the shares held in Adevinta (to whom eBay sold its Classified business), net cash holdings rose to $2.3 billion. With 532 million shares trading at $41 per share, the market value came in near $22 billion, with operating assets valued around $20 billion.

This made it so that operating assets traded at 8-9 times earnings, yet these earnings were adjusted, among others, excluding $600 million in stock-based compensation expenses. If we back this out, realistic earnings multiples for the operating assets came in around 11-12 times earnings.

That looked really cheap, yet the business was shrinking in terms of relevance and engagement, all of which made me cautious at the time.

Making Inroads

That conclusion has been too conservative with the benefit of hindsight, as shares rose to the $60 mark in 2024, the $80 mark in the summer of 2025, and even to a high around $100 in the fall. By now, shares have pulled back to $90 per share at this moment.

In the spring of last year, it was evident that eBay had seen an improvement in some operating performance. Total gross merchandise volumes for the year 2024 rose 2% to $74.7 billion, but growth accelerated throughout the year. Reported revenues rose by a similar near 2% to $10.3 billion for the year. GAAP operating profits came in at $2.3 billion, with GAAP earnings reported at $3.99 per share, as non-GAAP earnings were reported at $4.88 per share, that number up an impressive sixty-four cents on an annual basis.

Net cash holdings are down to just below a billion, mostly because of large share buybacks, the main purpose of the capital allocation scheme of the past decade.

The big gains were seen in 2025, as the operating performance revealed 2%, 4%, and 8% constant currency growth in both GMV and sales in the first three quarters of the year, respectively. The company attributes this to savvy usage of AI, as well as various other business initiatives.

Consequently, the company sees currency-neutral sales up around 6% to $11.0 billion in 2025, based on GMV growth of around 5% to levels just over $79 billion. Adjusted earnings are seen around $5.45 per share, with GAAP earnings seen just over $4.10 per share. With about three-quarters of the adjustments relating to stock-based compensation expenses, I see realistic earnings of $4.50 per share here, as I do not accept that adjustment. Continued buybacks resulted in the company having incurred a modest net debt load, now seen around half a billion.

The 467 million shares trading around the $90 mark grant equity a $42.0 billion equity valuation and slightly higher enterprise valuation. This made it so that valuations have risen to about 4 times sales, just below that, and with reported earnings around $4.50 per share, these shares trade around 20 times earnings. This makes it so that 120% share price returns over the past two years have largely been driven by valuation multiple inflation. Operating asset earnings multiples increased from around 11-12 times realistic earnings to 20 times, and only to a minor extent by earnings improvements and the impact of buybacks.

However, some momentum is definitely seen returning to eBay which has long stagnated but is really seeing 8% growth here, driven by improvement of the marketplace function. The composition of this engagement is interesting, as the number of active buyers is only up a percent to 134 million users, while GMV is up 10%, driven by strength in the US operations and thus driven by higher activity per user.

What Now?

The discussion above really shows what is going on. Based on a non-demanding valuation, amidst concerns about the platform's relevance, shares have seen a huge re-rating over the past two years. Concerns came after GMV rose just 2% in 2023 in an inflationary period, all while the number of active buyers was actually down. Lower engagement and activity was in part due to greater competition, including Facebook's Marketplace, among others.

This came after credits were due where they are due, as eBay has stepped up its game this year, with accelerating growth seen here. From a very low valuation base, any kind of accelerating business momentum deserves a re-rating. By now, shares have risen to 20 times realistic earnings. This looks reasonable for a business with very minimal net leverage, also given that the business has seen 8% growth in recent times. However, it seems hard to believe that 8% revenue growth (organic) seems attainable in the years to come.

Given all this, I feel no need to chase the shares after this great run-up. While the business is better and the valuation a re-rating seems more than deserved, I am cautious. After all, current growth momentum is welcomed, but any setbacks in this, which could easily happen, might easily put pressure on valuation multiples awarded to the business. This makes me cautious to build real confidence in the investment case yet, also as the re-rating has been rather aggressive.

I admit to too cautious an approach before, seeing no need to get involved just yet.

data.title: Powell Under Attack
data.description:
Deja vu, as the Fed's Chair, Jerome Powell, gave an announcement that the Fed (and he specifically) is under an investigation prompted by the executive office. The Fed was hit with DOJ subpoenas relating to their historic building renovations, which have cost some $2.5B so far, something President Trump has insisted was beefed up fraudulently. This saga has been ongoing all year, with President Trump continuing to volley insults and threaten to fire Chair Powell all through 2025. This investigation appears to be the next step forward in the threats.

The outcome that President Trump wants? Ostensibly, control of interest rate policy.

In the past, President Trump has said that he wants Powell out because the Fed has been too slow on rate cuts and that rates should be far lower today than they are. Back in June, President Trump had his press secretary present a note to the public on this matter, comparing the Fed Funds Rate to other countries and suggesting that the US should have a rate sub-1% immediately.

Powell, in his own statement, said that he believes this is tactical:

I have deep respect for the rule of law and for accountability in our democracy. No one—certainly not the chair of the Federal Reserve—is above the law. But this unprecedented action should be seen in the broader context of the administration’s threats and ongoing pressure.

This new threat is not about my testimony last June or about the renovation of the Federal Reserve buildings. It is not about Congress’s oversight role; the Fed through testimony and other public disclosures made every effort to keep Congress informed about the renovation project. Those are pretexts. The threat of criminal charges is a consequence of the Federal Reserve setting interest rates based on our best assessment of what will serve the public, rather than following the preferences of the President.

I have a feeling that my readers' stance on this issue will fall along political lines (as it often does when President Trump intercedes in monetary policy matters), but what won't be deniable is the impact on markets.

Treasuries Rise, Dollar Hurts

The core reaction to this kind of meddling was summed up in a joint letter written by the past three Fed chairs, Yellen, Bernanke, and Greenspan, "This is how monetary policy is made ⁠in emerging markets with weak institutions, with ‍highly negative consequences for inflation and the functioning of their economies ‌more ‌broadly."

Markets have become somewhat accustomed to these kinds of stunts by now, and the reactions are far more muted than they were back in June when Powell made those remarks to Congress that are now being called into question. Rhetoric then about firing Powell was just as bellicose, but the market reaction was far more extreme.

Equity markets were mixed, but this announcement did a few things. Notably, it snapped an uptrend in the dollar. Predictably, this dollar fall came at the same time as a jump in high-trust currencies like the Euro and Swiss Franc.

This was accompanied by a rise in interest rates along the entire curve, which tends to happen when the dollar weakens but also is a show of confidence in the government's management of the economy more broadly.

The US 30yr is now at 4.84%, still sitting around its highest levels in years.

The consequence of attacking the central bank's members and it as an institution is a loss of trust in both the central bank and the executive branch. Regardless of the side one falls on in who is right, Trump or Powell, they will leave the discussion likely having a worse opinion of the integrity of the other party. This distrust for either side squarely harms the credibility of the US monetary system as a whole. That creates demand for higher interest rates on Treasuries and less demand for the dollar.

This fight is one more reason for foreign countries to de-dollarize their treasuries, which is what I am most concerned about in this exchange. The dollar didn't take too bad of a hit, but gold and silver ran up quickly.

A Precious Metals Victory

The clear winner of this exchange is precious metals. On the day of the news, gold shot up over 2% and silver over 7%. They have been experiencing their own rallies in a broader sense, largely due to central bank buying, but this is another catalyst for foreign countries to get a move on and get rid of more dollars.

Investors flee to safety when things get tense, and bonds are precluded from this since they are trying to flee to safety away from the government. As long as this showdown is on between Powell and Trump, it is wise to be long precious metals; they already are and are likely to stay as the undisputed benefactor of any erosion of Federal Reserve independence.

It's still likely that Powell will resign his governorship when his term of Chair ends in May, but he could choose to stay on as a governor until January 2028. If he decides to stay, and the odds are looking better that he will following this incident, he could be a thorn in the administration's side for a while and drag on this issue through the courts, which could last longer than Jan. 2028.

Any future clashes are likely to be met with the same fate: an erosion of trust in the US by market players. That comes with all the trappings that one gets in emerging markets: weaker currency, higher rates for sovereign bonds, and a flight away from your currency and bonds into precious metals and other currencies.

Will This Change the Fed Decision?

To President Trump's chagrin, I find this move unlikely to motivate the Fed to move any faster on their current rate path. They have been very adamant that the executive branch does not decide monetary policy and that they will move at the pace they find best. Powell reiterated this in his statement on the investigation. I doubt this will motivate any change in their decision-making.

I still expect rates to be held at the next FOMC meeting, coming at the end of the month, as the Fed enters its next "wait and see" period. We are unlikely to get another rate cut, from my view, until at least April, if not June, when we have a new Fed chair.

Regardless of what happens with the investigation, Powell will step down as chair in May. Another Fed chair will be announced, with the front-runners being Kevin Warsh and Kevin Hassett, although I believe that Waller is the best candidate to promote both unity and dovishness, a win-win for both the FOMC and executive view. Although I'm willing to admit that he doesn't have great odds for a reason, President Trump's history and current actions with this investigation suggest that he does not care to please the FOMC and choose an insider.

Conclusion

Ultimately, I'm not sure what will come of the investigation into Chair Powell or the Fed's renovations; history with the Lisa Cook investigation tells us that it's unlikely that Powell would be removed from his role unless found criminally liable, but by the time any case could be fully made against him, he will be out of his role. The law simply doesn't move fast enough to fire him now, even if this one does stick.

I am sure that the longer this saga goes on, the less trust institutions and investors will have in the US. That leads to a weaker dollar overall, higher rates on Treasuries, and continued bullish pressure on precious metals.

The response to this event was fairly muted compared to what it was months ago when things like this happened. Perhaps investors, equities traders in particular, are getting used to it and are already pricing in that the investigation won't yield anything usable or that the Supreme Court will rule that Powell can stay in his position, like they did for Cook. It seems to me that this event was not the catalyst I expected, and the market is pricing in a retraction of this event. Either way, broken trust cannot be repaired as easily.

Thanks for reading.
